records.ety: [AS. smītan to smite, to soil, pollute; akin to OFries. smīta to smite, LG. smiten, D. smijten, G. schmeissen, OHG. smīzan to smear, stroke, OSw. & dial. Sw. smita to smite, Dan. smide to throw, Goth. bismeitan, to anoint, besmear; cf. Skr. mēd to be fat. The original sense seems to have been, to daub on, to smear. Cf. Smut.]
records.morph: [imp. Smote (smōt), rarely Smit (smt); p. p. Smitten (smt"t'n), rarely Smit, or Smote; p. pr. & vb. n. Smiting (smīt"ng).]

defs: To strike; to inflict a blow upon with the hand, or with any instrument held in the hand, or with a missile thrown by the hand; as, to smite with the fist, with a rod, sword, spear, or stone.
quotes: Whosoever shall smite thee on thy right cheek, turn to him the other also.
And David . . . took thence a stone, and slang it, and smote the Philistine in his forehead.

defs: To cause to strike; to use as an instrument in striking or hurling.
quotes: Prophesy, and smite thine hands together.
Saul . . . smote the javelin into the wall.

defs: To destroy the life of by beating, or by weapons of any kind; to slay by a blow; to kill; as, to smite one with the sword, or with an arrow or other instrument.
To put to rout in battle; to overthrow by war.
To blast; to destroy the life or vigor of, as by a stroke or by some visitation.
quotes: The flax and the barly was smitten.

defs: To afflict; to chasten; to punish.
quotes: Let us not mistake God's goodness, nor imagine, because he smites us, that we are forsaken by him.

defs: To strike or affect with passion, as love or fear.
quotes: The charms that smite the simple heart.
Smit with the love of sister arts we came.

defs: To strike; to collide; to beat.
quotes: The heart melteth, and the knees smite together.

defs: The act of smiting; a blow.
